headline: One waterfall. Twelve verticals. Built once.
subheadline: A scrollable GTMx strategy artifact for routing checkout-owning vertical SaaS platforms by monetization maturity, not by industry silo.
productSummary: Vertical Insure sells embedded, vertical-specific insurance into software platforms that already own a checkout. The platform earns revenue share on every policy while Vertical Insure carries carrier relationships, licensing, compliance, underwriting risk, and claims.
coreInsight: Vertical is the personalization variable. Monetization maturity is the waterfall. The same cascade can run across sports, pets, events, property, construction, travel, and future categories without rebuilding the campaign.

sampleValues.{{refund_clause_quote}}: All registrations are final after the season begins.
sampleValues.{{negative_review_quote}}: Paid $340, kid got injured, no refund.
sampleValues.{{jd_quote}}: Own new monetization products across payments and financial services

sections.label: Signal
sections.title: The buying signal is monetization maturity
sections.takeaway: A platform's vertical changes the noun in the email. Its checkout maturity decides the campaign.

sections.label: Waterfall
sections.title: One cascade routes the whole market
sections.takeaway: Every checkout-owning platform flows top to bottom: competitor widget, monetization hire, refund pain, payment rails, or catch-all.

sections.label: Tiers
sections.title: Each tier sells a smaller first step
sections.takeaway: Cold email should not ask for an integration. It should sell a teardown, model, margin comparison, or mock checkout.

sections.label: Campaigns
sections.title: Campaign angles ride on the same routing layer
sections.takeaway: The core seventeen campaigns become manageable once the routing layer decides which signal deserves the first touch.

sections.label: Email Copy
sections.title: Copy starts with public evidence
sections.takeaway: Every sequence opens with a scraped widget, job post, refund clause, payment rail, or checkout-specific idea.

sections.label: Launch
sections.title: Launch narrow, then widen the market
sections.takeaway: Start with the BuiltWith competitor list, add the refund scrape, turn on job triggers, then scale into payment rails and catch-all.

signalReasons: A competitor protection widget proves the category is already bought and visible at checkout.
A live monetization or embedded-finance hire proves non-core revenue is funded right now.
A strict refund clause proves acute customer pain using the prospect's own public words.
A payment facilitator proves they already chose to own money movement and clip transactions.
Every remaining checkout-owning platform still has protection demand happening off-platform.

sources.name: BuiltWith protection-widget lists
sources.delivers: Cover Genius, XCover, Refund Protect, Booking Protect, Protecht, Extend, Route, Seel, Redo, Rainwalk, and similar incumbent detections

sources.name: Capterra, G2, and GetApp directories
sources.delivers: Core market of transaction-owning vertical software platforms, tagged by category and checkout ownership

sources.name: Headless scrape of pricing, terms, refund policy, and help centers
sources.delivers: Refund language, checkout ownership, transaction fees, and customer-count claims

sources.name: BuiltWith payment facilitator lists
sources.delivers: Stripe Connect, Adyen for Platforms, Payrix, Finix, Stax, Tilled, and Infinicept detections

sources.name: LinkedIn Jobs and Google Jobs
sources.delivers: Live Head of Payments, Embedded Finance, Monetization, Fintech PM, and Partnerships requisitions

sources.name: Apollo and Crunchbase
sources.delivers: Catch-all market coverage, headcount tier, funding stage, and buyer mapping

tiers.name: Tier 1
tiers.label: Competitor Widget Takedown
tiers.trigger: A competitor protection widget is detected at checkout
tiers.pain: They bought a horizontal product built for parcels and bolted it onto a vertical workflow. One SKU, generic claims flow, thin revenue share, and no vertical-specific coverage.
tiers.valueProp: Industry-specific customization, not cookie-cutter insurance. Bundle multiple products per vertical, with Vertical Insure holding the carriers, licenses, and claims desk.
tiers.magnet: Product-fit and revenue-share teardown against their current widget

tiers.name: Tier 2
tiers.label: Active Intent
tiers.trigger: Live requisition for Head of Payments, Embedded Finance, Monetization, Fintech PM, or Partnerships
tiers.pain: They are about to spend headcount and quarters standing up non-core revenue. Insurance sits behind payments because they assume it requires licensing.
tiers.valueProp: No underwriting risk. Vertical Insure handles carriers, compliance, licensing, and claims while the platform rev shares.
tiers.dataSource: LinkedIn Jobs and Google Jobs scrape
tiers.magnet: Embedded-insurance revenue model for their vertical

tiers.name: Tier 3
tiers.label: Refund Leakage
tiers.trigger: A strict non-refundable clause appears in their terms or customers' registration pages
tiers.pain: Their customers hard-code no refunds because money is already spent on facilities, coaches, venues, or deposits. The result is chargebacks, disputes, and lost registrations.
tiers.valueProp: Insurance turns the refund fight into a claim. Partners see more bookings and more revenue per transaction.
tiers.dataSource: Headless scrape of terms, refund-policy pages, and Powered By footprints
tiers.magnet: Refund Leakage Teardown using their clause and customer count

tiers.name: Tier 4
tiers.label: Rails Already Built
tiers.trigger: Stripe Connect, Adyen for Platforms, Payrix, Finix, Stax, Tilled, or similar detected
tiers.pain: They already chose to own money movement and clip a spread, but they are not clipping the higher-margin protection attach on the same checkout.
tiers.valueProp: A no-cost, no-risk revenue generator with zero disruption to conversions.
tiers.dataSource: BuiltWith payment facilitator detection plus pricing-page checks
tiers.magnet: One-page comparison of processing spread against insurance commission

tiers.name: Catch-all
tiers.label: Owns the Transaction
tiers.trigger: Any remaining checkout-owning platform with at least eleven employees
tiers.pain: They monetize on subscription alone while their customers buy protection off-platform from brokers or PDF workflows.
tiers.valueProp: Protection built into the platform, with revenue share and no underwriting risk.
tiers.dataSource: Capterra/G2/GetApp category lists, Apollo, and Crunchbase

campaigns.name: Creative Ideas
campaigns.filter: Checkout-owning platforms without a stronger signal
campaigns.strategy: Crawl their site and propose three Vertical Insure products that attach to the exact checkout moment
campaigns.overview: The broadest motion sells curiosity instead of an integration.

campaigns.name: Mock Checkout Video
campaigns.filter: Platforms with a visible signup, booking, registration, or purchase flow
campaigns.strategy: Render the module inline inside their actual checkout and script a ninety-second walkthrough
campaigns.value: Kill conversion and engineering objections early
campaigns.overview: A visual first step for softer leads.

campaigns.name: Payments versus Insurance Margin
campaigns.strategy: Compare their payment spread against insurance commission on the same rails
campaigns.overview: The clean Tier 4 control because the targeting already carries relevance.

campaigns.name: Refund Clause Teardown
campaigns.filter: Strict refund clause or customer clauses found
campaigns.strategy: Quote the verbatim clause and combine it with customer count to price the leakage

campaigns.name: Competitor Widget Takedown
campaigns.strategy: Name the incumbent and identify the vertical-specific coverage it lacks
campaigns.value: Replace generic protection with vertical-fit bundles
campaigns.overview: The hottest list because category demand is proven.

campaigns.name: Hiring for Monetization
campaigns.filter: Live monetization, payments, fintech PM, or partnerships job
campaigns.strategy: Quote the job description's own words and offer a revenue model before the hire starts
campaigns.value: Launch insurance before the roadmap hardens
campaigns.overview: This is an always-on trickle, not a batch.

campaigns.name: Funding Trigger
campaigns.strategy: Reference round size, lead investor, and stated use of funds
campaigns.value: Board pressure to expand revenue per customer
campaigns.overview: Run after the core waterfall is stable.

campaigns.name: Conference Exhibitor Sweep
campaigns.filter: InsurTech, SaaS, sports tech, event tech, or veterinary exhibitor directories
campaigns.strategy: Scrape exhibitor lists, not live social hashtags

messages.subject: {{incumbent_widget}} at checkout
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, saw {{company_name}} runs {{incumbent_widget}} at checkout.

It sells one SKU, built for parcels, bolted onto {{vertical_noun}}.

Would it be fair to assume nobody has offered your {{customer_noun}} {{missing_coverage}} alongside it?

We build per vertical and bundle. WrestlingIQ ran Registration Cancellation and Gap Medical together. 11.5% of checkouts bought in month one.

Reply Y and I'll send the product fit and rev share teardown for {{vertical}}. No call.

messages.subject: threaded follow-up
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, the part most platforms miss with {{incumbent_widget}}.

You carried the integration cost. They kept the margin.

We rev share on every policy, and you never touch underwriting, licensing, compliance or claims. That sits with us.

SportsEngine stacked embedded services to 34% higher ARPU.

Two weeks of one developer to switch. Want the numbers for {{vertical}}?

messages.subject: cookie-cutter
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, insurance built for parcels does not know what a {{vertical_noun}} is.

It cannot price the risk, so it prices it generically. Your {{customer_noun}} feel that, and it shows up in the attach rate.

We build a product per vertical. Registration Cancellation and Gap Medical are different policies because a cancelled season and a torn ACL are different problems.

No need for a call. Reply yes and I'll send the {{vertical}} teardown. Should I send it to you, or whoever owns monetization?

messages.subject: redirect
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, if the protection widget at {{company_name}} is not yours to own, point me at whoever it belongs to and I'll go there instead.

Either way, appreciate the time.

messages.subject: {{customers_with_clause}} of your {{customer_noun}}
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, I pulled {{customers_checked}} {{customer_noun}} running on {{company_name}}.

{{customers_with_clause}} of them publish some version of "no refunds once the season starts."

Matt Sencenbaugh at WrestlingIQ put it plainly. If someone cancels at the last minute and the club has already paid, somebody eats it.

Right now that is your {{customer_noun}}, or their {{end_user_noun}}, and it lands in your support queue either way.

We embed Registration Cancellation at your checkout. Nobody eats it, and you rev share on every policy.

Want the list of clauses I found?

messages.subject: threaded follow-up
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, the refund policy is the symptom. Here is the number underneath it.

{{company_name}} serves {{customer_count}} {{customer_noun}}. At the attach rates our partners see, protection on those checkouts is a line of revenue you are not currently billing for, at a margin above what you make on payments.

You do not underwrite it. You do not license it. You do not handle a single claim.

Want me to run the math on {{company_name}} specifically?

messages.subject: who eats it
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, when a parent pulls their kid out of a season three weeks in, who eats the cost at {{company_name}}?

The club already paid for the field, the refs and the coaches. So either the club refunds and loses money, or it does not refund and loses the parent.

Both outcomes end up in your support queue.

There is a third option, and it pays you. Reply yes and I'll send the {{vertical}} model. Should I send it to you, or whoever owns monetization?

messages.subject: resource offer
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, last one from me.

Whether or not embedded protection is on the roadmap, I ran the refund leakage teardown for {{company_name}} anyway. Your clauses, your {{customer_count}} {{customer_noun}}, and what it adds up to annually.

Would it be useful if I sent it over?

messages.subject: {{jd_title}}
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, saw the {{jd_title}} req at {{company_name}}. The JD says "{{jd_quote}}."

Would it be fair to assume insurance sits third on that roadmap, behind payments and lending, because you assume it needs licensing?

It does not. We hold the carriers, the licenses, the compliance and the claims desk. You rev share.

WrestlingIQ shipped it in two weeks with one developer. 11.5% of checkouts bought protection in month one, 100% rollout at go live.

You could have it live before {{jd_title}} starts. Want the {{vertical}} revenue model?

messages.subject: threaded follow-up
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, on the {{jd_title}} search.

The payments spread is the obvious first project. It is also the thinnest margin your new hire will find.

Insurance rides the same rails and pays better. SportsEngine got to 34% higher ARPU stacking embedded services on top of what they already had.

We built the insurance layer for LeagueApps, Stack Sports, TeamSnap and Demosphere.

Want the model?

messages.subject: roadmap question
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, when you scope monetization at {{company_name}}, where does insurance land?

Most product leaders put it last, because the licensing looks like a nine month problem. That is the whole reason we exist. We carry the carriers, the licenses, the compliance and the claims. You carry the checkout.

Our partners grew 270% last year. Nobody on that list has an insurance team.

No call needed. Reply yes and I'll send the {{vertical}} revenue model. Should it go to you, or to {{jd_title}} once they start?

messages.subject: redirect
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, if monetization is not yours, tell me who owns it at {{company_name}} and I'll take it there.

Appreciate the time either way.

messages.subject: {{payfac_name}}
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, {{company_name}} runs {{payfac_name}}, so you already clip the transaction.

Insurance clips more, on the same rails. Zero underwriting risk. We hold the carriers, the licenses and the claims desk.

WrestlingIQ shipped it in two weeks with one developer. 11.5% of checkouts bought in month one.

Reply Y and I'll send the revenue model. No call.

messages.subject: threaded follow-up
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, three things about {{company_name}} that made me send this.

- You run {{payfac_name}}, so the rev share plumbing exists
- Your {{customer_noun}} pre-spend before they get paid
- Your pricing page monetizes seats, not transactions

Embedded protection turns the third one around without touching the first two.

Our revenue grew 150% last year doing exactly this. Worth a look?

messages.subject: question for {{first_name}}
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, what is the highest margin line on {{company_name}}'s P&L that is not subscription?

For most platforms that own a checkout, the answer should be insurance and almost never is. Payments got built because it was obvious. Insurance did not, because everyone assumes it means becoming an insurance company.

It does not. It means two weeks of one developer.

No call. Reply yes and I'll send the model. You, or whoever owns monetization?

messages.subject: resource offer
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, last note.

If this is not the quarter for it, I'll send the margin one pager anyway. Processing spread against insurance commission, for a platform the size of {{company_name}}.

Useful?

messages.subject: three for {{company_name}}
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, I was on {{company_name}}'s site and had three ideas for your checkout.

- {{vi_product_1}} at {{checkout_moment}}, protects the {{customer_noun}} who have already spent the money
- {{vi_product_2}} alongside it, because the bundle is what drives the attach rate, not the individual product
- {{vi_product_3}} for {{secondary_use_case}}

Of course, I wrote this without knowing your roadmap or your conversion targets.

WrestlingIQ ran the first two together. 11.5% of checkouts bought in month one, off two weeks of one developer's time.

If it is interesting, happy to send what attach rates look like in {{vertical}}.

messages.subject: threaded follow-up
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, your {{customer_noun}} are already buying this.

From a broker, off your platform, on a PDF, badly. None of it touches {{company_name}}.

We move it into your checkout, white labelled. You rev share on every policy and carry zero underwriting risk.

Partners grew 270% on our side last year. Is this on the roadmap at all?

messages.subject: {{vertical}} attach rates
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, quick story.

WrestlingIQ runs software for 150 wrestling clubs. Small platform, one developer to spare. Their clubs kept eating last minute cancellations because the money was already spent on mats, refs and gym time.

They embedded Registration Cancellation and Gap Medical at signup. Two weeks of work. 11.5% of checkouts bought protection in the first month, and the clubs stopped absorbing cancellations.

{{company_name}} sells to {{customer_noun}} with the same problem. Worth a look?

messages.subject: redirect
messages.body:
{{first_name}}, if protection at checkout is not something you own, let me know who does at {{company_name}}.

Otherwise I'll leave it here. Appreciate the time.

launchPlan: Launch Tier 1 first from the BuiltWith competitor-widget export. Run one variant until it clears enough volume.
Launch Tier 3 in parallel once the refund-policy and Powered By scrape is ready. This is the highest-personalization tier.
Turn on Tier 2 as an always-on trickle from live job posts, sent within fourteen days of the role going live.
Layer Tier 4 after Tiers 1 and 3 reveal whether margin, conversion, or risk-transfer language lands better.
Open the catch-all in week seven with a mock-checkout video and the winning broad value proposition.
Apply registration-season timing across all tiers so outreach lands six to ten weeks before the buyer's enrollment window.

thresholds.action: Borderline. Test one copy or lead-magnet variant.
Cut the tier or rework it from the trigger up.
